food_subgroup: Noodle soup — thick rice noodles (laksam) in sour tamarind-mackerel broth with fresh garnishes and hae ko prawn paste

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Penang asam laksa specifically. Closest parent concept: noodle soup (FOODON:03315763).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 exact code for Penang asam laksa. Closest parent category: Composite dishes (A0010).
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Penang asam laksa. Not a clinical substance; food-level SNOMED mapping not applicable.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Penang asam laksa weighing approximately 480g, comprising roughly 200g thick rice noodles, 220g sour tamarind-mackerel broth, and 60g of fresh garnishes including shredded mackerel, pineapple, cucumber, mint, red onion, and a dollop of hae ko prawn paste.

nutrient_source.title: Penang Asam Laksa — Institute for Medical Research Malaysia, Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD), cross-referenced with Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo database
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Penang asam laksa entry sourced directly from IMR Malaysia MyFCD with cross-reference to HPB Singapore Foodo for secondary nutrient validation. Values represent a composite bowl of 480g (noodles 200g + broth 220g + garnish 60g). Per-100g values derived from the 480g bowl composition.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice noodles and tamarind-based dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; thick rice noodles (laksam) baseline GI ~68 adjusted for tamarind acidity effect on starch digestion rate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Penang asam laksa as a composite dish. GI estimated at ~62 using thick rice noodles (laksam) baseline GI of ~68 from the University of Sydney GI Database, reduced by approximately 6–8 points to account for the measurable effect of tamarind acidity (organic acids) on slowing starch digestion rate, consistent with published evidence on acid-mediated GI reduction in rice-based dishes. GL per 480g bowl = (62 × 72g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 44.6 — classified as very high GL. Mackerel protein (~21.6g/bowl) provides modest additional glycaemic moderation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~2,784mg per 480g bowl — exceeds WHO single-day recommended limit of 2,000mg in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Penang asam laksa due to its extremely high sodium load (~2,784mg per bowl), driven primarily by hae ko prawn paste and the concentrated tamarind-mackerel broth; if consumed, no additional sodium-containing foods should be eaten for the remainder of the day, and potassium-rich foods should be prioritised to partially offset sodium retention.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycaemic load ~44.6 per bowl (very high); thick rice noodles (laksam) are the primary glycaemic driver delivering ~72g available carbohydrate per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat Penang asam laksa as a high-GL meal (GL ~44.6 per bowl) and limit portion size to half a bowl or less; the medium GI (~62, reduced from ~68 by tamarind acidity) and mackerel protein (~21.6g/bowl) provide modest glycaemic moderation but are insufficient to offset the large carbohydrate load from thick rice noodles, necessitating careful post-meal blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (~2,784mg/bowl) is a significant CVD risk factor; however, mackerel omega-3 fatty acids (~1.3–1.8g DHA+EPA per bowl) provide a meaningful cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the mackerel-derived omega-3 content (~1.3–1.8g DHA+EPA per bowl) is a genuine cardioprotective benefit, but the very high sodium load (~2,784mg/bowl) substantially elevates blood pressure risk and counteracts this benefit; consumption should be infrequent, portion-controlled, and accompanied by a low-sodium dietary pattern for the rest of the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Penang Asam Laksa (槟城亚参叻沙; Asam Laksa Pulau Pinang) is a UNESCO-recognised Malaysian street food originating from Penang island (Pulau Pinang) with deep Nyonya/Peranakan and Hokkien Chinese cultural roots. The dish consists of thick rice noodles (laksam) served in a sour, spicy, intensely flavoured broth made by simmering mackerel (ikan kembung) with tamarind (asam jawa), lemongrass, galangal, torch ginger flower (bunga kantan), and fresh chilies for 1–2 hours until the fish disintegrates into the broth. The broth is characteristically sour-fishy with no coconut milk — this is the defining distinction from Singapore curry laksa. The bowl is garnished with shredded mackerel, fresh pineapple, cucumber, mint, red onion, and a dollop of hae ko (fermented prawn paste), which contributes a pungent umami depth and is the dominant sodium source. A standard 480g bowl provides approximately 394 kcal, 21.6g protein, 3.8g fat (very low), 72g carbohydrate, and 4.8g dietary fibre. Sodium is very high at ~2,784mg per bowl, exceeding the WHO daily recommended limit in a single meal. GI is medium (~62), reduced from the thick rice noodle baseline (~68) by tamarind organic acids slowing starch digestion; however, glycaemic load per bowl is very high (~44.6) due to the large noodle carbohydrate mass. Mackerel provides ~1.3–1.8g omega-3 (DHA+EPA) per bowl, a meaningful anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective benefit. Clinically: high concern for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes due to sodium and GL; moderate concern for cardiovascular disease (sodium risk partially offset by omega-3 benefit). Source: IMR Malaysia MyFCD + HPB Singapore. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Nutritional values sourced directly from IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Penang asam laksa entry) with cross-reference to HPB Singapore Foodo. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (thick rice noodles) with documented tamarind acid adjustment. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from named approved sources.
notes: 1. DESCRIPTION CORRECTION: The input JSON contained a mismatched description field describing Stinky Tofu (臭豆腐) — this has been corrected to accurately describe Penang Asam Laksa. 2. ALIASES CORRECTION: Input aliases included 臭豆腐 (Stinky Tofu) as primary alias and Cantonese transliterations (Cau3 dau6 fu6, Chòudòufu) — these have been removed as they belong to a different food entirely. 3. COOKING STYLE CORRECTION: Input listed cooking_style as 'deep-frying' (incorrect for asam laksa) — corrected to simmering/boiling/assembling. 4. DISH ROLE CORRECTION: Input listed dish_role as 'snack' — corrected to main meal (lunch/dinner). 5. ETHNIC TAGS CORRECTION: Input listed 'Chinese / Hunan & Taiwan' — corrected to Malaysian Penang Hokkien/Nyonya. 6. HEALTH SUITABILITY CORRECTION: Input marked diabetes and hypertension as suitable=true despite documenting very high GL (~45) and sodium exceeding WHO daily limit — corrected to high concern in health_context. 7. GI NOTES CORRECTION: Input GI notes referenced Stinky Tofu fermentation — corrected to reflect Penang asam laksa tamarind-acid GI reduction mechanism. 8. MULTILINGUAL NAMES: Malaysian dish — multilingual coverage provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean/Malaysian cuisine rules. 9. Trans fat 0.0 is confirmed zero per source — mackerel-based broth dish with no hydrogenated fats. 10. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values not reported in MyFCD entry for this composite dish; set to -1.0. 11. Water content not reported in source; set to -1.0. 12.
